abstract:
This paper presents a state-of-the-art supervised statistical text classification system designed to detect sentiment in short, informal texts. The system addresses two distinct tasks: message-level sentiment detection (classifying the overall polarity of a tweet or SMS) and term-level detection (identifying the sentiment of a specific word or phrase within a given context). 

The core of the approach relies on a diverse set of features, including surface-form, semantic, and specialized sentiment features. A major contribution of this work is the development of high-coverage, tweet-specific sentiment lexicons generated automatically from a corpus of millions of messages containing emoticons and sentiment-bearing hashtags. Furthermore, the authors address the linguistic challenge of negation by developing separate lexicons to capture the distinct sentiment shifts of words in affirmative versus negated contexts. 

Evaluated against the SemEval-2013 competition datasets, the system ranked first in both the message-level and term-level tasks for Twitter data, and demonstrated strong cross-domain performance on SMS and movie reviews. Ablation experiments highlight the critical role of the automatically generated lexicons, which contributed gains of up to 6.5 absolute percentage points. The systematic analysis confirms that corpus-based empirical estimation of negated terms significantly outperforms traditional polarity-reversal heuristics.
